study_notes.title: Maintaining Good Posture and Dental Health
study_notes.introduction: The skeletal and muscular systems work together to provide structure, support, and the ability to chew food. Maintaining correct body posture and regular dental hygiene are essential habits for a healthy musculoskeletal system.

body_posture.definition: The habitual way a person positions their body while sitting, standing, or moving (walking/running).
importance: Keeps the spine in its natural 'S-shape', preventing permanent bone deformities.
Prevents muscle strain, fatigue, and chronic backaches.
Allows internal organs (stomach and lungs) sufficient space to function efficiently.
Promotes a confident, smart, and healthy appearance.
rules_for_good_posture: Sitting: Sit upright with the back against the chair's support, both feet flat on the floor.
Standing: Keep the head high, chin level, and shoulders relaxed.
Walking: Keep the back straight and swing arms rhythmically.
Sleeping: Use a firm surface/mattress to prevent spinal twisting.
Carrying bags: Distribute weight evenly across both shoulders to prevent scoliosis (sideways spinal curve).

postural_disorders.scoliosis: A permanent, abnormal sideways curvature of the spine (often caused by uneven weight-bearing, like carrying bags on one shoulder).
postural_disorders.kyphosis: An excessive outward curvature of the upper back, commonly known as a hunchback.
postural_disorders.lordosis: An excessive inward curve of the lower back, often called a 'swayback'.
postural_disorders.consequences: Deformed bones, chronic pain, restricted breathing due to chest compression, and indigestion.

teeth_anatomy: Enamel: The hard, outer white layer; the hardest substance in the human body.
Dentine: The bone-like layer under the enamel that forms the bulk of the tooth.
Pulp Cavity: The living center containing nerves (for pain) and blood vessels (for nutrients).
Cementum: The tissue anchoring the tooth root to the jawbone.

types_of_teeth.incisors: Chisel-shaped; used for biting and cutting.
types_of_teeth.premolars: Broad with ridges; used for crushing.
types_of_teeth.molars: Large and flat; used for grinding food.

dental_hygiene_habits: Brushing: Twice daily (morning and before sleep) to remove plaque.
Flossing: Using a string to clean hidden gaps where a brush cannot reach.
Diet: Reducing sugary foods (which bacteria turn into acid) and eating foods rich in Calcium and Phosphorus for enamel strength.
Fluoride: Using fluoridated toothpaste to harden enamel against acid decay.

nutrition_for_the_system: Protein: Essential for the growth and repair of muscle fibers and tissues.
Calcium and Phosphorus: Mineral salts that provide the rigidity and strength for bones and tooth enamel.
Vitamin D: The 'key' that allows the body to absorb calcium. Lack of this causes soft, bending bones (Rickets in children, Osteomalacia in adults).
study_tips_for_ple: Scoliosis = Side-to-side curve; Kyphosis = Hunchback; Lordosis = Swayback.
A toothbrush cannot reach between teeth; only floss can!
Bacteria + Sugar = Acid; Acid + Enamel = Cavities (Dental Caries).
Sitting hunched over compresses the chest, making it harder for the diaphragm to pull air into the lungs.
Bones and teeth are both 'living' structures because they contain blood vessels and nerves.
